my_sentence: I don't know what the brownout.
correction: I don't know what a brownout is.
explanation: An embedded question keeps statement word order and needs the verb 'is.'
correct_examples: I don't know what a brownout is.
Do you know what this word means?

my_sentence: I can read some news on internet, but it didn't affect me.
correction: I can read some news on the internet, but it doesn't affect me.
explanation: Use present tense for the current effect. The teacher explicitly recast 'didn't' as 'doesn't'; 'on the internet' is an assistant note.
correct_examples: I read the news on the internet, but it doesn't affect me.
The weather doesn't affect my work.

my_sentence: Some health product said...
correction: Some health products are said to be...
explanation: The product receives the claim, so use the passive 'are said to be' with a plural noun.
correct_examples: Some health products are said to be effective.
These medicines are said to reduce pain.

my_sentence: Some product look very beautiful, but you find it so small and bad.
correction: Some products look very beautiful, but you find them to be small and bad.
explanation: Use plural 'products' after 'some,' plural agreement, and the object-complement pattern 'find them to be.'
correct_examples: Some products look attractive online.
I found them to be poorly made.
context: Products that look different from their advertisements.

my_sentence: Sometimes you find their smartphone. It is so nice and cheap.
correction: Sometimes you find their smartphones are so nice and cheap.
explanation: Complete the first clause instead of cutting the idea into a fragment. The teacher repeatedly prompted the joined sentence.
correct_examples: Sometimes you find their smartphones are very cheap.
You may find the products look attractive.

my_sentence: When you buy it, you find that's not true.
correction: When you buy it, you find that the claim isn't true.
explanation: Use 'that' to introduce the complete object clause after 'find.'
correct_examples: You may find that the claim isn't true.
I found that the product was useless.

my_sentence: Their products can help you became white.
correction: Their products can help you become white.
explanation: After the modal 'can' and 'help,' use the base form 'become,' not past-tense 'became.'
correct_examples: The cream claims it can help your skin become lighter.
Exercise can help you become stronger.

my_sentence: I never seen that before.
correction: I have never seen that before.
explanation: Use the present perfect for an experience up to now: 'have never seen.'
correct_examples: I have never seen that product before.
She has never used lip balm.

my_sentence: Some clothes that made by cotton...
correction: Some clothes that are made of cotton...
explanation: A passive relative clause needs 'are': the clothes 'are made of' a material.
correct_examples: Some clothes are made of cotton.
The jacket is made of synthetic fabric.

my_sentence: Just like go to a restaurant to eat.
correction: Just like going to a restaurant to eat.
explanation: After 'like' in this comparison, use the gerund 'going.'
correct_examples: It is just like going to a restaurant.
Shopping there is like visiting a market.

my_sentence: I don't think their product are bad.
correction: I don't think their products are bad.
explanation: The plural verb 'are' requires the plural count noun 'products.'
correct_examples: I don't think their products are bad.
Their clothes are affordable.
context: Judging a global clothing company's quality.

my_sentence: Some companies had too much chemical in it.
correction: Some companies put too many chemicals in them.
explanation: Use plural 'chemicals' and the plural pronoun 'them' for multiple products. The teacher explicitly prompted 'in them.'
correct_examples: Some companies put chemicals in their products.
The foods have additives in them.

my_sentence: You spend many money and get nothing.
correction: You spend much money and get nothing.
explanation: 'Money' is uncountable, so use 'much,' not 'many.' The teacher explicitly corrected the quantifier.
correct_examples: You spend much money and get nothing.
They don't have much money.

my_sentence: If they sell more than medicine...
correction: If they sell more medicine...
explanation: Use 'more + noun' for a larger quantity. 'More than' introduces a comparison, which is not intended here.
correct_examples: Hospitals earn more if they sell more medicine.
We need more information.

my_sentence: You need to ask for doctor to get it.
correction: You need to ask a doctor for it.
explanation: Ask the person directly, then use 'for' before the requested thing.
correct_examples: You need to ask a doctor for a prescription.
I asked my manager for help.

my_sentence: Cancer patients need this medicine to reduce hurt.
correction: Cancer patients need this medicine to reduce pain.
explanation: Use the noun 'pain' for physical suffering; 'hurt' is normally a verb or adjective in this pattern.
correct_examples: The medicine reduces pain.
Cancer patients may need pain relief.

my_sentence: I usually buy some medicine for sleeping and headache.
correction: I usually buy some medicine for sleep and headaches.
explanation: Use plural 'headaches' for a recurring condition. The teacher repeatedly prompted the plural.
correct_examples: I buy medicine for headaches.
This medicine helps with sleep.
context: Medicine Jack buys during the year.

my_sentence: They just want to take advantage of people who don't have logic.
correction: They want to take advantage of people who are naive and lack critical thinking.
explanation: The teacher recast the description with 'naive.' 'Lack critical thinking' is more natural than 'don't have logic.'
correct_examples: Scammers target people who are naive.
Some viewers lack critical thinking.

my_sentence: They are cheated.
correction: They are being cheated.
explanation: Use the present continuous passive for an ongoing act: 'are being cheated.'
correct_examples: The customers are being cheated.
People are being misled online.
context: People paying for fake money-making methods.

my_sentence: They find they can't use them.
correction: They find that they can't use them.
explanation: Use 'that' to introduce the complete object clause after 'find.'
correct_examples: They find that the methods don't work.
I found that the information was useless.

my_sentence: I can use it to instead of coffee.
correction: I can use it instead of coffee.
explanation: Do not put 'to' before the fixed phrase 'instead of.'
correct_examples: I use cola instead of coffee.
She walked instead of driving.
context: Using cola as a coffee substitute.

my_sentence: That will be not a problem.
correction: That will not be a problem.
explanation: Put 'not' immediately after the modal 'will.'
correct_examples: That will not be a problem.
It will not cost much.
context: Paying for care in old age.

my_sentence: You can join some hobby clubs, like go fishing and play cards.
correction: You can join some hobby clubs, like going fishing and playing cards.
explanation: After 'like' when listing activities, use parallel gerund forms. The teacher explicitly prompted 'going fishing.'
correct_examples: They enjoy going fishing and playing cards.
The club offers swimming and hiking.

my_sentence: There are many activities that you can join.
correction: There are many activities that you can do.
explanation: You join a club or group, but you do an activity. The teacher explicitly supplied 'do.'
correct_examples: There are many activities you can do.
You can join a fishing club.

my_sentence: I usually buy some water from the biggest company.
correction: I usually buy bottled water from the biggest company.
explanation: Use 'bottled water' to specify commercially packaged drinking water. The teacher supplied the phrase.
correct_examples: I usually buy bottled water.
They sell glass-bottled water.

my_sentence: Glass water is higher two to four times than plastic bottled water.
correction: Glass-bottled water is two to four times more expensive than plastic-bottled water.
explanation: Use 'times more expensive than' for a price comparison, and use compound adjectives for the bottle types.
correct_examples: Glass-bottled water is twice as expensive.
This bottle costs more than that one.
context: Comparing glass and plastic bottled water.

my_sentence: It's more safe, I think.
correction: It's safer, I think.
explanation: The comparative form of the short adjective 'safe' is 'safer.' The teacher explicitly corrected it.
correct_examples: This building is safer.
An inside door feels safer than an outside door.
